coredata.dc:title: A novel self-enhancement NCNDs-BPEI-Ru nanocomposite with highly efficient electrochemiluminescence as signal probe for ultrasensitive detection of MTB
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jicui
Zhang, Yue
Chai, Yaqin
Yuan, Ruo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel self-enhanced Ru(Ⅱ)-based nanocomposite (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru), synthesized by covalently connecting nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) and tris (4,4′-dicarboxylicacid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) dichloride (Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2) through branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI), could be used as an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal probe for ultrasensitive detection of mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB) DNA fragment. Without any extraneous coreactants, NCNDs-BPEI-Ru could generate an ultra-strong initial ECL signal on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (signal-on) due to the coreaction from BPEI and NCNDs to Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2. Then, self-assembled DNA nanotubes embedding methylene blue molecules (MB-DNANTs) were modified on the nanocomposite to effectively quench the ECL intensity of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru owing to the quenching effect of MB for Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2 (signal-off). Finally, the 3D DNA nanomachine, formed by a DNA-functionalized magnetic beads (DNA-MBs) could transform trace target MTB into massive output DNA, and the MB-DNANTs could be disintegrated to get ECL signal recovery (signal-on) due to hybridization of output DNA and the S2 in DNANTs. Therefore, the proposed biosensor exhibited predominant performance for the detection of MTB DNA fragment with a low detection limit of 1.4 aM, which showed great application value in the detection of biomarkers in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence biosensor
Self-enhancement
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
DNA nanotubes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Self-assembly of DNANTs and preparation of MB-DNANTs
UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the nanomaterials
Electrochemical behaviors of the ECL biosensor
Specificity and stability of the MTB biosensor
Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor for MTB detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21974108 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM graph of the NCNDs (A) and inset of A: HRTEM graph; AFM graph of the NCNDs (B).
UV–vis absorption spectroscopy for different materials (A); XPS analysis of BPEI (B), NCNDs-BPEI (C) and NCNDs-BPEI-Ru (D).
3D ECL surface image of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru. (A) The ECL spectra of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru. (B).
ECL intensities of the proposed biosensor with different Nb.BbvCI (A) and Exo III concentration(B). The added error bars referred to the measurement standard deviation of three repeated experiments.

CV responses (A) of (a) bare GCE, (b) (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion)/ GCE， (c) S0/ (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion)/ GCE, (d) HT/ S0/ (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion) /GCE, (e) DNANTs/ HT/ S0/ (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion)/ GCE and (f) output DNA/ DNANTs/ HT/ S0/ (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion)/ GCE; the PAGE analysis of the 3D DNA Nanomachine (B) and the DNANTs (C).

The ECL intensity of MTB biosensor with several distinctive interferents and the added error bars referred to the measurement standard deviation of three repeated experiments (A); reproducibility of the proposed strategy (B); stability study of this biosensor incubated with 1 nM (C) and 100 aM (D) MTB under continuous cyclic potential scans in 2 mL PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4).

ECL signals of the proposed biosensor with various DNA concentrations: (a) 50 aM, (b) 100 aM, (c) 1 fM, (d) 10.0 fM, (e) 100 fM, (f) 1 pM, (g) 10.0 pM, (h) 100 pM and (i) 1 nM (A). Calibration curve for MTB determination (B).
Preparation of MB-DNANTs (A), principle of 3D DNA nanomachine (B), construction of the proposed MTB biosensor (C) and the self-enhancement mechanism of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru (D).

head.ce:title: A novel self-enhancement NCNDs-BPEI-Ru nanocomposite with highly efficient electrochemiluminescence as signal probe for ultrasensitive detection of MTB

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel self-enhanced Ru(Ⅱ)-based nanocomposite (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru), synthesized by covalently connecting nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) and tris (4,4′-dicarboxylicacid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) dichloride (Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2) through branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI), could be used as an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal probe for ultrasensitive detection of mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB) DNA fragment. Without any extraneous coreactants, NCNDs-BPEI-Ru could generate an ultra-strong initial ECL signal on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (signal-on) due to the coreaction from BPEI and NCNDs to Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2. Then, self-assembled DNA nanotubes embedding methylene blue molecules (MB-DNANTs) were modified on the nanocomposite to effectively quench the ECL intensity of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru owing to the quenching effect of MB for Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2 (signal-off). Finally, the 3D DNA nanomachine, formed by a DNA-functionalized magnetic beads (DNA-MBs) could transform trace target MTB into massive output DNA, and the MB-DNANTs could be disintegrated to get ECL signal recovery (signal-on) due to hybridization of output DNA and the S2 in DNANTs. Therefore, the proposed biosensor exhibited predominant performance for the detection of MTB DNA fragment with a low detection limit of 1.4 aM, which showed great application value in the detection of biomarkers in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Tuberculosis (TB) caused by mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB) was one of the deadliest infectious diseases globally [1–3]. As an valid biomarker for early diagnosis of TB, sensitive and rapid detection of specific DNA fragment (a DNA fragment of MTB) showed considerable significance [4]. Currently, several methods for detecting DNA fragments have been used for clinical diagnosis of TB, such as traditional Ziehl-Neelsen sputum smear microscopy [5], fluorescence microscopy [6] and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) strategy [7,8]. Although the above methods have been widely used so far, the stability and sensitivity ought to be further improved to meet the requirements of ultrasensitive DNA detection. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) was redox induced luminescence phenomenon in which excited states were generated by the movement of high-energy electrons near the working electrode [9,10]. At present, all aspects of ECL research, such as new ECL systems, sensing strategies and microscopy, have been developed rapidly [11–13]. In addition, ECL stood out from various analytical methods due to its low background noise, excellent sensitivity and superior reproducibility, which possessed important value in the bioanalytical field such as the clinical detection of DNA, proteins and cells [14–18]. In the traditional coreactant ECL system, the ECL response of emitter could be improved by adding coreactant to the detection base solution [19]. However, the intermolecular reaction between luminophore and coreactant with long electronic transmission distance increased energy loss and impeded electronic transmission, which limited the ECL efficiency of the luminophore. Meanwhile, small molecule coreactants had some adverse effects on the environment due to the shortcomings of corrosivity, toxicity and volatility [11,20–25]. Hence, it was challenging to explore high ECL efficient and environmentally friendly emitters. In this work, by using tris (4,4′-dicarboxylicacid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) dichloride (Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2) to simultaneously covalently connect with good biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) and branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI) as coreactants of Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2, we successfully synthesized a double self-enhanced Ru(Ⅱ)-based nanocomposite (NCNDs-BPEI-Ru) with amazingly high ECL efficiency owing to the intramolecular coreaction from the amino groups of BPEI and NCNDs to Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2, which facilitated construction of ultrasensitive ECL biosensor. Therefore, the environmentally friendly nanocomposite NCNDs-BPEI-Ru with excellent ECL performance possessed significant reference value for expanding the application of ECL in various fields, especially for ultrasensitive detection of disease markers in sensing application.

In this study, an ultrasensitive ECL biosensor was developed for target (a DNA fragment of MTB) detection via employing the double self-enhanced NCNDs-BPEI-Ru with strong ECL emission as signal probe and methylene blue labeled DNA nanotubes (MB-DNANTs) as signal amplifier. As depicted in Scheme 1A, three kinds of DNA single strands (S1, S2 and S3) were applied to the assembly of 3D DNANTs by one-pot annealing according to the principle of complementary base pairing, so that MB could be inserted into double-strand DNA of 3D DNANTs via π-π stacking interactions. Simultaneously, single-strand DNA R1 was complementary to the exposed part of S2 in order to immobilize MB-DNANTs on the electrode surface. Next, the amplification strategy of the target DNA was carried out by the following steps (Scheme 1B). As the introduced target DNA competed to bind with protection DNA (PD), walker DNA (WD) was released and hybridized with support DNA (SD) to form the helical WD-SD containing the corresponding recognition sequences of the Nb.BbVCI nicking endonuclease, which could generate abundant output DNA. Additionally, the nanocomposite NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/ Pt nanoparticles (PtNPs)/Nafion was coated on the clean GCE to produce a super-strong initial ECL signal (signal-on) (Scheme 1C). After sulfhydryl-modified DNA S0 were modified on the nanocomposite via PtS bonds, MB-DNANTs were connected to the surface of the GCE by hybridization between R1 and S0 for achieving a conspicuous “signal-off” state due to the obvious signal quenching effect of MB on Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. Then, introduced output DNA could trigger the toehold strand displacement reaction to hybridize with the toehold of S2 in DNANTs, which caused the gradual disintegration of MB-DNANTs and the release of MB from the electrode surface. In addition, the output DNA could be released by Exonuclease III cutting to drive the next cycle to realize the ECL signal striking recovery (signal-on), and further achieving signal amplification. Therefore, the proposed strategy could be an available reference for exploring novel ECL nanomaterials and ultrasensitive detection of other biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembly of DNANTs and preparation of MB-DNANTs
ce:section.ce:para: The DNA nanotubes (DNANTs) were successfully acquired by slightly modifying the previous reference [26], which was presented in Scheme 1A. Three kinds of DNA single strands (S1, S2 and S3) were used to build DNANTs by one-pot annealing from 95 ℃ to 37 ℃ in TAE buffer for 20 h. After mixing single-stranded DNA R1 complementary to part of S2 and the above solution and incubating at 37 °C for 2 h, the DNANTs were successfully assembled. Then, the mixture solution of the DNANTs (50 μL) and 3 mM methylene blue (MB) (100 μL) was slightly shaken at 37 °C for 1 h to make sure MB was fully embedded in DNANTs. Finally, MB-DNANTs were obtained.

The preparation process of the ECL biosensor was described in Scheme 1C. At first, referring to previous literature [27], the glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 4 mm) was seriously polished with alumina slurry and then fully washed to acquire a mirror-like surface. Next, 10 μL NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion solution was modified on the clean GCE, drying at 37 ℃ for 3 h. The self-enhancement nanocomposite NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion produced a strong and stable initial ECL response on the electrode owing to the excellent film forming properties of Nafion. Afterward, 10 μL DNA probe S0 was immobilized on the NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion via PtS bonds overnight in refrigerator. Then, 10 μL 1-hexanethiol (HT) solution (1 mM) as the blocking agent was dripped on the modified electrode at 37 °C for 30 min. Thus, 10 μL MB-labeled DNA nanotubes (MB-DNANTs) could be connected on the GCE through the hybridization reaction of S2 contained in the DNANTs and S0 at 37 ℃ for 2 h. Subsequently, the well-prepared output DNA and 3 U Exonuclease III (Exo III) were incubated on the GCE at 37 °C for 1 h to induce the Exo III-aided cycle before carrying the ECL test.

The morphology of the nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) was explored by the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in Fig. 1A, the NCNDs with the average diameter of about 4 nm possessed monodispersity and uniformity, which had the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm corresponding to the (102) lattice fringes of graphitic (sp2) carbon, suggesting its high crystallinity. The Fig. 1B showed that the height of the NCNDs was about 3.0 nm, indicating its single layer form. The results of characterization were consistent with references [28], which indicated that the NCNDs were prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the NCNDs-BPEI-Ru was characterized by UV–vis spectra. As observed in Fig. 2A, two distinct peaks of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ were located at 305 nm and 475 nm, which were attributed to the characteristic π → π* transition of CC and the n → π* transition of CN. There was no absorption peak for BPEI, while an obvious absorption peak at 239 nm was observed for NCNDs. After NCNDs and Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ were orderly cross-linked onto BPEI, the peak shape of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru was similar with that of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, and the characteristic peak of NCNDs at 210 nm still existed. Due to the cross-linking reaction and overlay effect, the three absorption peaks of as-obtained NCNDs-BPEI-Ru were blue shifted at 210 nm, 302 nm and 465 nm, respectively. Therefore, the successful synthesis of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru was verified.

The XPS analysis results of BPEI, NCNDs-BPEI and NCNDs-BPEI-Ru were shown in Fig. 2B–D. We could observe that there were characteristic peaks of C 1s (286 eV), N 1s (398.79 eV) and O 1s (532.31 eV) in three nanomaterials. Besides, the peak of Ru 3p at 485.96 eV was observed and the peak at 286 eV attributing to the binding energy of Ru 3d was overlapped with the peak of C 1s (Fig. 2D), illustrating that Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ was cross-linked onto NCNDs-BPEI. In brief, the results of the above UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and XPS analysis certified the successful synthesis of NCNDs-BPEI-Ru.

The ECL spectra was applied for specific ECL analysis of nanomaterial, which was obtained by measuring NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/Nafion modified GCE in 2.0 mL PBS (0.10 M, pH 7.4) under the scanning potential range of +0.2 V–+1.25 V. As displayed in Fig. 3A, B, NCNDs-BPEI-Ru had a strong ECL emission peak at 620 nm, and its initial potential was + 1.10 V, which was consistent with the ECL emission wavelength and emission potential of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, demonstrating that the illuminant of the gained nanocomposite was Ru(dcbpy)3 2+.

Appropriate concentration of Nb.BbvCI and Exo III was significant to develop the excellent performance of the proposed biosensor. Therefore, two key experimental parameters including the concentration of Nb.BbvCI and Exo III were investigated. From Fig. 4A, the ECL signal gradually went up with the growth of Nb.BbvCI concentration (1–5 U/μL) until the Nb.BbvCI concentration was over 5 U/μL. Thus, the optimal concentration of Nb.BbvCI was 5 U/μL. As shown in Fig. 4B, with the ascending concentration of Exo III (0.5–2.0 U/μL), the ECL intensity gradually increased. When the concentration of Exo III was more than 2 U/μL, the ECL intensity changed slowly. Therefore, 2 U/μL was used as the optimal Exo III concentration during the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammogram (CV) measurement was executed to corroborate the successful construction of the MTB biosensor. As seen in Fig. 5A, there was a pair of perfect [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox peak of the bare electrode (curve a). When the GCE was embellished by NCNDs-BPEI-Ru/PtNPs/Nafion, the peak value significantly decreased, which could be explained by the Nafion’s obstructive effect on electron transfer (curve b). After ordinal modification of S0 and HT, the redox peak gradually decreased on account of incremental electron repulsion and steric hindrance (curves c and d). Then, MB-DNANTs were immobilized on the resulted GCE, and a markedly enhanced redox peak was obtained, because MB was conducive to the electron transmission (curve e). Eventually, as the import of output DNA and Exo III on the electrode, the redox peak value dropped again (curve f). It could be attributed to that DNANTs on the GCE were collapsed completely, so that MB was released from the electrode surface. The above results were consistent with the expectation to prove that construction of the ECL biosensor has been successful.

The polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) experiment was performed to verify the results of DNA reaction. The production process of output DNA was shown in Fig. 5B. Four bright bands (lanes a-d) were observed for support DNA (SD), walker DNA (WD), protection DNA (PD) and target, respectively. When PD was added to the mixed solution of WD and SD for incubation, a new clear band (lane e) appeared and the band of SD had not changed, indicating the formation of the product double-stranded DNA (WD-PD). After sequentially adding target DNA to the above solution, WD was released from WD-PD and then hybridized with SD to form the helical WD-SD (lane f). Finally, when Nb.BbvCI was added to the previous solution, numerous output DNAs were acquired (lane g).

The DNANTs assembly process also was confirmed by the PAGE analysis. As was shown in Fig. 5C, three clear bands (lanes a-c) were observed for S1, S2 and S3 with similar molecular weight and lane d was the R1 with a smaller molecular weight. Besides, the DNA S0 moved fastest due to the smallest molecular weight (lane e). Moreover, after five types of single-strand DNAs (S1, S2, S3, R1 and S0) were self-assembled into DNANTs, the DNANTs with largest molecular weight moved slowest (lane f) and AFM characterization of DNANTs was shown in Fig. S1, which suggested the formation of DNANTs. Ultimately, after the addition of the output DNA, the structure of DNANTs were disrupted completely and the lane g was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the MTB biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed biosensor presented an excellent selectivity by evaluating target DNA (10 pM) and other interferences including smDNA (1 nM), dmDNA (1 nM), tmDNA (1 nM) and the mixture solution, respectively. As seen in Fig. 6A, the ECL response of three interfering DNA was as low as the blank group. However, the amazingly high ECL emission of the target DNA and the mixture solution was recorded, indicating that the proposed ECL biosensor possessed extraordinary selectivity. Additionally, Fig. 6B indicated the reproducibility of the proposed biosensor. For the target (1 pM) detection, the relative standard deviations (RSD) of intra-assay and inter-assay were 2.35% and 1.52%, respectively, which showed the excellent stability of the target biosensor. Besides, the stability of the biosensor was speculated by the cyclic potential scanning for 13 cycles and the result was displayed in Fig. 6C, D. All the above results proved the superior performance of the constructed ECL biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor for MTB detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum experimental conditions, the ECL response of target DNA with gradient concentration was detected to check out the performance of the proposed biosensor. As the concentration of target DNA increased from 50 aM to 1 nM, the ECL intensity constantly increased ( Fig. 7). The regression equation was I = 5726.31＋1866.32 lg c with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9974 (I was the ECL intensity and c was the concentration of target DNA) and the detection limit was 1.4 aM. Besides, the added error bars referred to the measurement standard deviation of three repeated experiments. Furthermore, preliminary application result of the ECL biosensor was listed in Table S2. Importantly, by comparing the proposed biosensor with the methods reported in previous literature (shown in Supporting Information Table S3), our approach embraced extraordinary performance for ultrasensitive biological determination.

In this work, an innovative ECL biosensor with high sensitivity and reproducibility was proposed for MTB ultrasensitive detection on the basis of self-enhancement NCNDs-BPEI-Ru as signal probe coupling with enzyme-assisted DNA walker and the MB-DNANTs as signal amplifier. The nanocomposite NCNDs-BPEI-Ru possessed striking ECL signal originated from the double self-enhancement from the amine groups of BPEI and NCNDs to Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2, which demonstrated extremely high ECL efficiency. Besides, as a powerful means of nucleic acid amplification, 3D DNA walker could transform trace target DNA into plentiful output DNA, realizing ultrasensitive detection of target DNA. Hence, the proposed strategy performed a great application potentiality in bioanalysis and early disease diagnosis.

Jicui Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yue Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yaqin Chai: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ruo Yuan: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21974108).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131252.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potential- and color-resolved electrochemiluminescence of polymer dots for array imaging of multiplex microRNAs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Wang, L. Chen, W. Chen, H. Ju, Potential- And Color-Resolved Electrochemiluminescence of Polymer Dots for Array Imaging of Multiplex MicroRNAs, Anal. Chem. 93 (2021) 5327–5333.

Shen, Facile Preparation of WO3−x Dots with Remarkably Low Toxicity and Uncompromised Activity as Co-reactants for Clinical Diagnosis by Electrochemiluminescence, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 16747–16754.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Voci, R. Duwald, S. Grass, D.J. Hayne, L. Bouffier, P.S. Francis, J. Lacour, N. Sojic, Self-enhanced multicolor electrochemiluminescence by competitive electron-transfer processes, Chem. Sci. 11 (2020) 4508–4515.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spatially selective imaging of cell–matrix and cell–cell junctions by electrochemiluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, P. Zhou, W. Fu, L. Ding, W. Guo, B. Su, Spatially Selective Imaging of Cell–Matrix and Cell–Cell Junctions by Electrochemiluminescence, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 60 (2021) 11769–11773.
S. Liu, H. Yuan, H. Bai, P. Zhang, F. Lv, L. Liu, Z. Dai, J. Bao, S. Wang, Electrochemiluminescence for Electric-Driven Antibacterial Therapeutics, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 140 (2018) 2284–2291.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual enhancement of gold nanocluster electrochemiluminescence: electrocatalytic excitation and aggregation-induced emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Peng, Z. Huang, H. Deng, W. Wu, K. Huang, Z. Li, W. Chen, J. Liu, Dual Enhancement of Gold Nanocluster Electrochemiluminescence: Electrocatalytic Excitation and Aggregation-Induced Emission, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 9982–9985.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Versatile electrochemiluminescence and electrochemical “on-off” assays of methyltransferases and aflatoxin B1 based on a novel multifunctional DNA nanotube

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ge, Y. Zhao, C. Li, G. Jie, Versatile Electrochemiluminescence and Electrochemical “on-Off” Assays of Methyltransferases and Aflatoxin B1 Based on a Novel Multifunctional DNA Nanotube, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 3546–3554.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Li, Z.T. Liu, J.Y. Li, A.Y. Chen, Y.Q. Chai, R. Yuan, Y. Zhuo, Enzyme-free Target Recycling and Double-Output Amplification System for Electrochemiluminescent Assay of Mucin 1 with MoS2 Nanoflowers as Co-reaction Accelerator, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 10 (2018) 14483–14490.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, Y. Chen, M. Liang, L. Xu, S. Qi, H. Chen, X. Chen, Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 9846–9852.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jicui Hu is a MS candidate in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. Her main research interests are electrochemiluminescence and biosensors.
Yue Zhang is a doctoral candidate in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. Her main research interests are electrochemiluminescence and biosensors.

Yaqin Chai is a professor of chemistry in Southwest University, China. She received a Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Southwest Normal University (China) in 2004. Her main research interests are the bioelectrochemical sensors. and chemical electrochemical sensors.

Ruo Yuan graduated from Hunan University (China) in 1991. He received his Ph.D. in analytical Chemistry from Hunan University in 1994 for the thesis devoted to chemical sensors. In 1995 he pursued postdoctoral research at the lab of Prof. H. Yoneyama in Osaka University, Japan and in 1998 he worked as a senior research scientist at City University of Hong Kong. In the interim, he devoted to the research on electrochemical sensor, nanomaterial sensor and DNA chip. Since 1999 he has been working as a professor of chemistry in Southwest University, China. His current research interest involves chemical sensor, electrochemical biosensor and electrochemiluminescence biosensor.
